quote: the property has two structures, and on the second structure, we have a roof a perfect roof to put a solar energy system on because it's it's a pretty large space, you know, covering probably around a thousand square feet, and it's, it's also all perfectly south facing.
stewart_read: Greg volunteers a high-value qualifier unprompted: a second structure with a large, south-facing roof he's already identified as ideal for solar. This is not a casual aside — it's Greg doing the rep's qualification work for him. Brandon's response is to ask about the referral program, which is procedurally reasonable but misses the moment entirely. The two-structure detail resurfaces at 08:43 when Greg mentions a HERS score for one structure, confirming this is a material lead characteristic, not a throwaway comment.
alternative_hypothesis: Brandon may have been holding the two-structure detail for the specialist to develop — but there is no evidence of this; he never references it again.
coaching_implication: Show Brandon this moment in the 1-on-1. Ask him: 'What did Greg just tell you at 01:41, and what did you do with it?' The correct move is to anchor on the detail — 'A thousand square feet, south-facing — that's actually a strong setup. Our specialist is going to want to see that specifically.' This validates Greg's research, builds anticipation for the appointment, and ties the booking to something concrete Greg already cares about.

quote: we'd also would wanna know on the, you know, the the the cost the cost sharing program that if there's any, like, you know, monies available to apply for to, to offset some of the costs of of installation as well as, you know, low interest loan programs and things like that or leasing options.
stewart_read: Greg is asking about financing structures — closer-tier content. Brandon's response ('that's what we will go over with you') is a procedurally correct deferral, but it's delivered without any acknowledgment of Greg's sophistication. The strategic read: Brandon is correctly holding scope. The reactive read: the deferral is flat and generic, which makes it feel like Brandon doesn't know the answer rather than that he's appropriately routing the question. The distinction matters for Greg, who has already signaled he's done his homework.
alternative_hypothesis: Brandon may have been deliberately brief to avoid scope creep — a correct instinct, but the execution undersells the deferral.
coaching_implication: The scope discipline is right; the delivery needs work. Coach Brandon on the difference between 'I don't know' deferral and 'that's exactly what the specialist is built for' deferral. Suggested framing: 'You clearly know this space — the financing structures, the incentive stacking, that's exactly what our specialist maps out in the session. That's their whole job.' Same boundary, different signal to the customer.

quote: So the idea would be if we have the solar, you know, we could we could then it doesn't really matter if it's less efficient because we have the solar system to provide the energy.
stewart_read: Greg has just done the objection inversion himself — he's reframed his inefficient heating system from a liability into a reason solar makes even more sense. This is a flip opportunity the rep walked past entirely. Brandon's next line (06:09) is 'Right. Okay. So who do you have for the utility company?' — a procedural pivot that ignores the logic Greg just handed him. The correct move is to land on Greg's own framing: 'Exactly — and that's actually the argument for sizing the system to cover the inefficiency rather than fixing it. That's the kind of math the specialist will run for you.'
coaching_implication: This is a textbook flip opportunity that Greg generated without any prompting. Brandon's job was to catch it and amplify it. Show Brandon this moment and ask: 'What did Greg just do here?' Then walk through what it looks like to land on a customer's own logic rather than moving past it. Per coaching_philosophy.objection_inversion, zero instances of this technique have been observed across the corpus — this call is a clean example of the opportunity being available and unused.

quote: we wanna go solar to be more renewable than we were. We also wanna lower our carbon footprint. There's there's a number of reasons. Right? We wanna save some money, of course, but we also, in addition to saving money, we're also, you know, wanna be more renewable in the way we're living.
stewart_read: This is the deepest motivation disclosure on the call — Greg is volunteering his full value stack: environmental values, cost strategy, and a specific rationale for solar over HVAC repair. The verify.interest_in_solar item is designed to surface exactly this. Brandon never asked for it; Greg offered it anyway. The coaching gap is not that Brandon failed to extract it — it's that he did nothing with it. There is no acknowledgment of the environmental motivation, no tie-back to it in the button-up, and no mention of it in the text follow-up promise (which was never made). Per text_after_call, the tieback to interests is a required element of the post-call text.
coaching_implication: Greg handed Brandon the tieback material for the post-call text and the button-up anchor. Neither was used. In the 1-on-1, ask Brandon: 'What did Greg tell you he actually cares about?' Then show him how that answer should have appeared in the button-up ('I've got you on for the 22nd to look at what a system on that south-facing roof would do for your energy footprint') and in the text. This is a concrete, low-effort improvement with direct impact on show rate.

quote: Great. We will set a tentative appointment for Wednesday, the twenty second at 01:00. When we get closer to the day, we'll we will reach out the day before just to confirm with you if that time still works.
stewart_read: Brandon's button-up is missing four of the six required elements from button_up: no one-hour block stated (he mentioned it earlier at 09:40 but not in the confirmation), no phone-appointment specification, no computer/no-driving anchor, no 'is that fair' verbal handshake, and no text follow-up promise. The 'we'll reach out the day before to confirm' framing is also softer than the schema's confirmation standard — it introduces an opt-out moment rather than locking the appointment. Additionally, Greg's conditional ('if it's cost effective, we would move on it by the summer') is live and unaddressed at close.
alternative_hypothesis: Brandon may have judged that Greg's self-directed engagement made a full button-up unnecessary — but the schema's required elements exist precisely because engaged customers no-show too.
coaching_implication: Walk Brandon through the button_up checklist against this call's close. The 'reach out the day before to confirm' line is the specific one to address — it's an invitation to cancel rather than a commitment anchor. The 'is that fair' handshake is the highest-leverage missing element for show rate. This is a mechanical fix, not a judgment call.

quote: But you mentioned you're referred by someone? Because we have a great referral program that did they get installed with solar with us?
stewart_read: Brandon spends approximately 90 seconds (02:14–03:15) collecting the referrer's name and spelling for the referral program. This is a time-burn that interrupts the verify flow at a moment when Greg was actively disclosing motivation. The referral program detail is an administrative task that could have been handled at the end of the call or via the post-call text. The opportunity cost is that Greg's momentum from the 01:41 two-structure disclosure was broken by a spelling exercise.
alternative_hypothesis: Brandon may have been following a required referral-capture protocol — if so, the timing is still coachable even if the capture itself is required.
coaching_implication: Flag the sequencing: referral capture is fine, but not at the cost of breaking a customer's disclosure momentum. Coach Brandon to note the referrer's name quickly and defer the spelling/details to the end of the call or the post-call text. 'I'll make sure Steve gets credit for the referral — let me grab his details at the end so we don't lose your train of thought.'

quote: I do have someone in the area who can meet with you. As soon as tomorrow, what day would work best for you and you have to take a look at evaluation?
stewart_read: Brandon moves to booking without confirming who else is on the title. Greg mentioned 'we' throughout the call (01:15 'we were referred,' 09:54 'You wanna do tomorrow or it's not a good day? Or when we come back?') — the plural pronoun is consistent and suggests a co-decision-maker. The verify.title_co_owners item is hard-required, and per protocols.spouse_decision, a co-decision-maker must be on the appointment. Brandon books without asking. The 'when we come back' at 09:54 is the clearest signal — Greg is checking with someone else before committing to tomorrow.
alternative_hypothesis: Brandon may have read 'we' as a royal we or a household reference rather than a co-decision-maker signal — but the schema requires the question regardless.
coaching_implication: The 'when we come back' at 09:54 is the moment to flag in the 1-on-1. Ask Brandon: 'Who is the we?' The correct move at that moment is: 'Sounds like you want to check with someone — is there someone else who should be on the call with you on the 22nd?' Per protocols.spouse_decision, this is a procedure question, not a persuasion question. Missing it puts the appointment at risk if a co-decision-maker surfaces at the closer stage.

quote: it's not something we need to move on immediately, but it's something we're interested in. If it's cost effective, we would move on it, you know, by the summer.
stewart_read: Greg closes with an explicit condition: cost-effectiveness. This is the only remaining barrier between interest and commitment, and Brandon does not address it. The correct move is not to argue the condition but to tie the appointment itself to resolving it: 'That's exactly what the session on the 22nd is for — you'll have the actual numbers in front of you, and you can make that call.' Instead, Brandon moves directly to calendar logistics, leaving the condition live. This is the handoff brief's most important unresolved item.
alternative_hypothesis: Brandon may have judged that pressing on the condition would create resistance — but acknowledging it and tying the appointment to its resolution is not pressure; it's alignment.
coaching_implication: Show Brandon the sequence: Greg states a condition at 10:06, Brandon responds with calendar logistics at 10:15. Ask: 'What did Greg just tell you, and what did you do with it?' The coaching point is that acknowledging the condition and tying the appointment to resolving it strengthens the booking rather than threatening it. This is a one-sentence fix with direct impact on show rate and closer preparation.
